coredata.dc:title: Pea isolate protein/β-cyclodextrin/CQDs microcapsules improve stability and in vitro sustained release of fish oil

Tian, Fang
Xu, Yongjing
Li, Yujin
Guan, Weiliang
Lu, Chenyang
Hu, Sheng
Cai, Luyun
coredata.dc:description: Fish oils (FOs) are rich in omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, which provide several health benefits. However, their oral bioavailability and stability are often limited due to the oxidation of polyunsaturated fatty acids, which degrade the oil's nutritional and sensory properties over time. In this study, FO microcapsules were prepared using pea protein isolate (PPI), β-cyclodextrin (β-CD), and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as wall materials through a freeze-drying method. The results demonstrated that the use of PPI/β-CD/CQDs composite significantly improved the encapsulation efficiency, stability, and antioxidant properties of the microcapsules, suggesting a promising approach for enhancing the shelf-life and health benefits of fish oil. Additionally, the peroxide value (POV) and thiobarbituric acid (TBA) values of the microcapsules increased with temperature and storage time. Notably, the incorporation of CQDs markedly improved the antioxidant properties of the microcapsules, thereby extending their storage stability. In vitro gastrointestinal digestion simulations demonstrated that microencapsulation facilitated the absorption of beneficial substances in fish oil within the human gut. This study offers valuable insights into the development of more efficient fish oil microcapsules for potential applications in food and pharmaceutical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pea protein
β-cyclodextrin
Carbon quantum dots
Microencapsulation
Lipid oxidation
In vitro release

Changes of POV and TBA of microcapsules with different wall materials during fish oil storage

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Turbidity curves and ζ-potential of PPI solutions under the influence of different pH values (A); Turbidity curves of PPI-β-CD(B); Effect of wall-core mass ratio on encapsulation efficiency and loading rate (C). Effect of adding amount of CQDs on encapsulation efficiency, Particle size and ζ-potential (D).
Effect of wall materials of different compositions on particle size and potential of microcapsules(A); Effect of adding amount of CQDs on encapsulation efficiency(B).
Morphological structure of fish oil microcapsules with different wall materials PPI(A), β-CD(B), PPI/β-CD(C), PPI/β-CD/CQD(D) observed by SEM.
Changes of POV(A) and TBARS(B) of microcapsules with different wall materials during fish oil storage.
Simulated changes in particle size(A), ζ-potential(B) and FFA release rate(C) in the digestive system during the digestion process.

head.ce:title: Pea isolate protein/β-cyclodextrin/CQDs microcapsules improve stability and in vitro sustained release of fish oil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fish oils (FOs) are rich in omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, which provide several health benefits. However, their oral bioavailability and stability are often limited due to the oxidation of polyunsaturated fatty acids, which degrade the oil's nutritional and sensory properties over time. In this study, FO microcapsules were prepared using pea protein isolate (PPI), β-cyclodextrin (β-CD), and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as wall materials through a freeze-drying method. The results demonstrated that the use of PPI/β-CD/CQDs composite significantly improved the encapsulation efficiency, stability, and antioxidant properties of the microcapsules, suggesting a promising approach for enhancing the shelf-life and health benefits of fish oil. Additionally, the peroxide value (POV) and thiobarbituric acid (TBA) values of the microcapsules increased with temperature and storage time. Notably, the incorporation of CQDs markedly improved the antioxidant properties of the microcapsules, thereby extending their storage stability. In vitro gastrointestinal digestion simulations demonstrated that microencapsulation facilitated the absorption of beneficial substances in fish oil within the human gut. This study offers valuable insights into the development of more efficient fish oil microcapsules for potential applications in food and pharmaceutical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The small yellow croaker (Larimichthys polyactis) is a popular seafood known for its tender flesh, pleasant flavor, and high nutritional value. In recent years, it has also been used to produce surimi, which generates approximately 50 % by-products, including fish heads and guts. Improper disposal of these by-products not only wastes valuable resources but also poses environmental risks (Chen et al., 2023). To address these issues, sustainable approaches that promote the stepwise utilization of by-products and processing waste have gained attention, adding value and improving profitability. Notably, recent studies have shown that by-products of croaker processing, such as those used in ceviche, are rich in EPA, DHA, and omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids. These fatty acids offer several functional benefits, including promoting infant brain and liver development and reducing the risk of cardiovascular disease, inflammation, and cancer (Shahidi & Ambigaipalan, 2018). However, the nutritional quality and sensory characteristics of fish oils degrade over time due to the oxidation of polyunsaturated fatty acids, which also impacts their bioavailability. To mitigate this issue, microencapsulation has been employed as an effective technique to enhance the chemical stability of fish oils. This method involves enclosing fish oil within one or more encapsulating materials, isolating it from external factors while enabling targeted release under specific conditions. Additionally, the use of natural or synthetic antioxidants is a common strategy for protecting fish oil (Katouzian & Jafari, 2016). Although synthetic antioxidants are effective, there are safety concerns associated with them and consumers are increasingly wary of synthetic additives in food. As a result, microencapsulation remains a promising solution to balance stability, safety, and functionality in fish oil applications.

Wall materials play a critical role in the storage stability, protection, and controlled release of microcapsules, directly influencing their encapsulation efficiency. Microcapsules consist of core materials, which contain the active ingredient, and wall materials that encapsulate and protect the core (Geranpour et al., 2020). In recent years, plant proteins have gained popularity as wall materials across the food, pharmaceutical, and cosmetic industries, driven by the growing demand for sustainable and eco-friendly products (Samadian et al., 2022). Among plant proteins, pea protein isolate (PPI) has shown great potential for use as a wall material due to its excellent emulsification properties, affordability, low allergenicity, and wide availability. For example, Song et al. (2023) utilized the freeze-drying method to create microcapsules of tomato seed oil using PPI, purple tannin, and chitosan as the encapsulating materials. Their study demonstrated that the microcapsules significantly improved the oxidation stability of tomato seed oil. β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) is made up of 7 glucose molecules connected by a −1, 4-glucoside bond. Due to its ideal cavity size, excellent biocompatibility, and low cost, β-CD is the most widely used natural cyclodextrin for producing microcapsules (Ghorbanzade et al., 2022). Erfani et al. (2022) used β-cyclodextrin and sodium caseinate to encapsulate corn cinnamon essential oil and found that microencapsulated ether cinnamon essential oil was effective in slowing down its oxidative changes and prolonging the shelf life of walnut kernels during storage (Erfani et al., 2022). The combination of PPI and cyclodextrin as a wall material can effectively slow down the oxidation process of the oil, but there are also problems such as large particle size, weak chemical stability, and low embedding rate, so it is necessary to introduce new substances to improve the situation.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have a high concentration of hydroxyl functional groups on their surface, including carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups and exhibit excellent hydrophilicity, environmental protection, photostability, chemical stability, high selectivity, low cost, low toxicity, and UV resistance (Yadav et al., 2023). Due to the high concentration of functional groups in the CQDs themselves, their incorporation into mixtures of polysaccharides and proteins is effective in improving stability through the formation of additional hydrogen bonds via carboxyl groups; Sani et al. incorporated CQDS into mung bean protein-pectin matrix to develop a thin film for optimizing the structural properties of real cardamom extracts. Sohrab et al. (2021) demonstrated that CQDs-Alginate-Gelatin integrated into microcapsules functioned as scaffold enhancers for mesenchymal stem cell differentiation, and that their presence markedly increased the viscosity and morphological stability of the capsules compared to those without CQDs. Their findings revealed that the incorporation of CQDs served as a physical nanofiller and enabled the formation of hydrogen bonds, electrostatic interactions, and hydrophobic interactions with proteins in the wall material, thereby enhancing the structural stability of the microcapsule wall.

Most commercial wall materials for microcapsules are composites of polysaccharides and proteins. However, these materials have certain drawbacks, including large particle sizes, limited chemical stability, and low encapsulation efficiency. The incorporation of CQDs, known for their small particle size, simple preparation, and controlled-release properties, can enhance the physical characteristics of wall materials, improve microcapsule stability, and extend shelf life (Calabrese G et al., 2021). Moreover, To overcome the limitations of conventional encapsulation methods, freeze-drying has emerged as a promising alternative due to its low-temperature processing, which preserves the structural and functional integrity of sensitive bioactives. Unlike spray drying, freeze-drying prevents thermal degradation and allows better control over the morphology and stability of the resulting microcapsules, reducing some active compounds still exposed on the surface of particles (Luciana D G et al., 2019). To date, the use of a PPI/β-CD/CQDs composite as a wall material for microcapsules has not been reported. Investigating the interactions between PPI, β-cyclodextrin (β-CD), and CQDs is a promising approach to improving the stability and preservation efficacy of encapsulated fish oil. However, there is still a lack of studies on the application of CQDs in combination with polysaccharides and proteins for encapsulating fish oil. This presents an exciting opportunity for future research in this area.

Therefore, this study aimed to investigate the changes in physicochemical properties of fish oil microcapsules under varying temperature, measuring the POV and TBA of the fish oil microcapsules. Additionally, the in vitro release behavior of the microcapsules was examined in gastric, intestinal, and oral fluids. This paper not only provides experimental data on the use of PPI/β-CD/CQDs as composite wall materials for fish oil encapsulation but also offers valuable theoretical insights to support the broader industrial application of fish oil microcapsules. The findings have important social and economic implications by promoting more effective preservation techniques for fish oil.

The small yellow croaker scrap oil in this experiment was extracted from the processing by-products of small yellow croaker by freeze-drying pre-treatment combined with an enzymatic process. Pea protein (85 %) was procured from Tianmen Hengchang Chemical Co., Ltd., while soluble starch, potassium iodide, and β-cyclodextrin were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Simulated oral saliva and porcine pancreatic lipase (enzyme activity: 30,000 U/g) were supplied by Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. All other reagents were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals used in the study were of analytical grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and properties of fish oil microcapsule

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots were synthesized through the Maillard reaction, a simple and efficient method known for producing CQDs with high surface functionality. Initially, 0.6 g of glucose and 0.6 g of L-lysine were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water via ultrasonic treatment (300 W 5min). The resulting solution was then heated in a microwave (700 W) for 10 min, during which its color shifted from colorless to pale yellow and eventually solidified, indicating the formation of carbon dots. These carbon dots were redissolved in a small amount of deionized water, followed by centrifugation (10,000 r/min for 10 min) to remove solid precipitates. The supernatant was further filtered using a 0.22 μm disposable syringe filter to eliminate any remaining solid impurities. The filtrate was transferred into a dialysis bag and dialyzed in deionized water for 24 h. Finally, the purified carbon dots were freeze-dried at −40 °C to obtain them in solid form (Liang et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PPI and β-CD stock solution
ce:section.ce:para: A 2 % (w/v) PPI solution was prepared by dissolving 10 g of pea protein isolate (PPI) in 500 mL of ultra-pure water, stirring the mixture at room temperature for 2 h. The pH was adjusted to 12 using 0.1 M NaOH, and the solution was left to stand for 30 min. The protein was then unfolded and refolded by heating the solution in a constant temperature water bath at 88 °C for 30 min, followed by immediate cooling to room temperature and pH adjustment to 7 b y 0.1 M HCl. Insoluble impurities were removed by centrifugation at 1600g for 15 min (Zhang et al., 2020).

A 2 % w/v β-CD solution was prepared by dissolving 10 g of β-CD in 500 mL of water. The solution was stirred continuously at room temperature for 24 h to ensure complete saturation of β-CD, which was confirmed by the presence of visible undissolved particles and no further change in solubility (Tavares et al., 2021). Both solutions were stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of optimal ratio and for complex coacervation between fish oil, PPI, PPI/β-CD and CQDs
Preparation of PPI/β-CD mixture solution and determination of optimal ratio
ce:section.ce:para: The PPI and β-CD stock solutions were blended to achieve PPI-to-β-CD ratios of 1:1, 2:1, 4:1, 6:1, 8:1, and 10:1 (w/v), while maintaining the total biopolymer concentration at 2.00 %. The optical density (OD) value (measured by absorbance at 600 nm), pH-dependent turbidity, and ζ-potential of the PPI/β-CD mixtures were evaluated across a pH range of 2–10 at 0.5-unit intervals. Additionally, the OD value and ζ-potential of PPI alone were measured at same pH conditions to examine the effects of β-CD on the solution. The impact of the presence or absence of β-CD on these parameters was systematically investigated (Rios-Mera et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of optimal ratio between PPI/β-CD and FO
ce:section.ce:para: The PPI/β-CD/FO mixture was homogenized at 10,000 rpm for 3 min at room temperature, then pre-frozen at −80 °C for 48 h. The vacuum degree was maintained at 0.1 mbar, the condenser (cold trap) temperature was set at −54 °C, and the shelf temperature was controlled at −10 °C. The total freeze-drying time was 25 h. The resulting microcapsules were stored in a desiccator in 4 °C for future use (Thomas et al., 2025).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of optimal ratio in CQDs adding
ce:section.ce:para: In subsequent steps, varying amounts of CQDs were added to the PPI/β-CD solution, as determined by the PPI/β-CD to achieve different PPI/β-CD to CQDs mass ratios (100:0, 100:1, 50:1, 20:1, 10:1, 5:1, 3:1). The mixture was continuously stirred for 2 h at 1200 rpm. The resulting PPI/β-CD/CQDs solution was then blended with fish oil in the appropriate ratio, homogenized at 10,000 rpm for 3 min at room temperature, and freeze-dried to form PPI/β-CD/CQDs/FO microcapsules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of encapsulation efficiency and payload
ce:section.ce:para:
To determine the surface oil content of the microcapsules, 1.5 g of microcapsule powder (mass m) was weighed and mixed with 30 mL of petroleum ether (boiling range: 30–60 °C). The mixture was gently shaken for 5 min, then extracted and filtered. The residue was rinsed with an additional 15 mL of petroleum ether and filtered again. The combined filtrates were transferred to a pre-weighed round-bottom flask (mass m1), and the petroleum ether was evaporated using a rotary evaporator. The flask was then dried at 105 °C to a constant mass (mass m2). The surface oil mass fraction (W1) was calculated using the formula in eq. (1).
(1)
W 1
=
(
m 2
−
m 1
)
m
× 100 %

To determine the total oil content of the microcapsules (W2), 1 g of fish oil microcapsule product (mass m3) was accurately weighed. After fully dissolving the sample in 20 mL of hot water, 40 mL of a petroleum ether-ethanol mixture was added for thorough extraction. The extraction process was repeated twice under identical conditions to ensure exhaustive extraction. Completeness of extraction was confirmed by the absence of a significant increase in oil yield after the second extraction. The combined extracts were transferred into a pre-weighed round-bottom flask (mass m4). After evaporating most of the solvent using a rotary evaporator, the residue was dried in an oven at 105 °C to a constant mass. The flask was then cooled to room temperature in a desiccator and weighed again (mass m5). Finally, the encapsulation efficiency (EE) and payload of the fish oil microcapsules were calculated using standard equations (3) and (4).
(2)
W 2
=
(
m 5
−
m 4
)
m 3
× 100 %
(3)
Encapsulation
efficiency =
W 2
−
W 1
W 2
× 100 %
(4)
Payload =
W 2
−
W 1
m
× 100 %

The particle size of the samples was measured using a laser granulometer, and the ζ-potential was analyzed by dynamic light scattering. Prior to analysis, freshly prepared composite agglomerates were diluted to 100 times their volume with deionized water and dispersed thoroughly using ultrasound (5 kHz) in an ice bath for 5 min. The refractive indices of the oil droplets and deionized water were 1.43 and 1.33, respectively. The temperature during measurements was maintained at 25 ± 0.5 °C. All measurements were performed in triplicate (Sarkar et al., 2009; Zhang et al., 2020).

The morphological characteristics of the fish oil microcapsules were analyzed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) to elucidate the relationship between the product properties and their microstructure. A small amount of microcapsule powder was sprinkled onto a sample platform coated with double-sided adhesive tape and gently pressed with a capillary to ensure some of the powder adhered to the tape. The surface powder was then scraped off with a blade, and any excess microcapsules were blown away. The sample was coated with a platinum film by gold sputtering using an ion sputtering instrument at a current of 10 mA. The morphology and structure of the microcapsules were then examined using SEM (Sarkar et al., 2009; Zhang et al., 2021).

The chemical structure of PPI, β-CD, FO, CQDs, PPI/β-CD, PPI/β-CD/CQDs, coacervates, and SPEO microcapsules was analyzed using a Nicolet iN10 infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Each sample was mixed with KBr at a weight ratio of 1:50, ground into fine powder, and pressed into transparent tablets using a high-pressure clamp. The spectral data were collected over the wavenumber range of 400–4000 cm−1, with 64 scans performed per sample. The sample spectra were obtained by subtracting the air spectrum (Lan et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different humidity, temperatures, on stability of fish oil microcapsule
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the effect of different storage temperatures on the oxidation degree of the microcapsules, POV was measured spectrophotometrically. Microcapsules (1.5 g) were mixed with iso-octane and isopropanol (2:1, v/v) and vortexed thoroughly for 3 s, followed by centrifugation at 6500 rpm for 3 min at room temperature. The supernatant was collected and mixed with a FeCl3 solution (prepared by combining 0.132 mol/L BaCl2 and 0.144 mol/L FeSO4, then centrifuged at 6500 rpm for 3 min at room temperature), and potassium thiocyanate solution (40 %, w/v). The mixture was diluted to 5 mL using a methanol solution (2:1, v/v) and vortexed for 3 s. After incubation at room temperature for 20 min, protected from light, the absorbance at 510 nm (A) was measured.

TBA measurements were conducted following the method of Hao et al. (2022), with slight modifications. Microencapsulated samples were mixed with 25 mL of distilled water and 25 mL of a 10 % trichloroacetic acid solution. The mixture was homogenized at 12,000 rpm for 2 min and then filtered after standing for 30 min. Next, 10 mL of the filtrate was combined with 10 mL of a 0.02 mol/L thiobarbituric acid solution and heated in a water bath at 80 °C for 40 min. The absorbance at 532 nm was then measured using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2600 A, Shimadzu, Japan). The TBA values were expressed as the malondialdehyde (MDA) content in mg/kg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of in vitro release characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: Simulated gastric fluid was prepared by first creating a diluted hydrochloric acid solution by mixing 23.4 mL of hydrochloric acid with 100 mL of water. Then, 16.4 mL of this diluted solution was added to 800 mL of water, followed by 10 g of pepsin. The mixture was shaken thoroughly, and water was added to bring the total volume to 1000 mL. The pH was then adjusted to 2.0 (Kuck et al., 2017).

Simulated intestinal fluid was prepared by dissolving 3.2 g of potassium phosphate monobasic in 250 mL of distilled water and adjusting the pH to 6.8 using a 0.1 mol/L sodium hydroxide solution. Next, 10 g of trypsin was dissolved in an appropriate amount of distilled water, and this trypsin solution was mixed thoroughly with the pH 6.8 potassium dihydrogen phosphate solution. Distilled water was then added to bring the total volume to 500 mL, with the final pH adjusted to 6.8. The mixture was incubated at 37 °C with continuous stirring at 100 rpm for 2 h.

According to the method reported by Qiu et al. (2015), 20 mL of microcapsule recovery solution was mixed with 20 mL of simulated oral saliva to form a final mixture containing 3 % fish oil by mass, with the pH maintained at 6.8. The mixture was thoroughly mixed and incubated at 37 °C for 10 min on a shaking table at 200 rpm. Following this, 20 mL of the product digested in the simulated oral saliva was combined with simulated gastric juice in a 1:1 vol ratio. The pH of the mixture was adjusted to 2.5 using 1 mol/L NaOH solution, and it was incubated at 37 °C for 2 h on a shaking table at 200 rpm.

Next, 30 mL of the simulated gastric juice digest was placed in a clean beaker, and the pH was raised to 7.0 using 1 mol/L NaOH. Then, 3.5 mL of 187.5 mg/mL bile salt, 1.5 mL of CaCl2 solution, and NaCl solution were added to simulate the small intestinal fluid. After adjusting the pH to 7.0, 2.5 mL of lipase was quickly added to initiate the reaction. The pH change of the reaction was monitored using a pH meter, and the solution was titrated with 0.05 mol/L NaOH to maintain the pH at 7.0. The volume of NaOH used was recorded. The same digestion procedure was carried out with a control emulsion that contained no fish oil.

All measurements were conducted in triplicate and the results were presented as mean ± standard deviation. Using IBM SPSS Statistics 20 for processing and analysis and Origin 8.0 for plotting, the experiment's results were visualized. p < 0.05 was considered to be significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimal parameters for complex agglomeration of PPI/β-CD
ce:section.ce:para: In the analysis of protein-polysaccharide complex coacervation, the interaction between biopolymers with opposing charges is primarily driven by electrostatic forces, with pH being a key factor influencing this interaction. The ζ-potential results from the PPI/β-CD complex highlight the effect of pH on the protein's charge density and the subsequent formation of coacervates. As shown in Fig. 1 (A), at pH levels above 6.5, the PPI solution remains clear and stable due to the relatively high absolute ζ-potential values, indicating that electrostatic repulsion is sufficient to prevent aggregation. As the pH decreases to around 6, the reduction in the absolute ζ-potential signals a weakening of repulsive forces, which likely leads to increased turbidity, reflecting the onset of aggregation. This transition from negative to positive charge near the isoelectric point (around pH 4.5) is crucial, as it marks the point where protein-protein interactions dominate over repulsive forces, resulting in significant aggregation and turbidity. As the pH continues to drop, the PPI solution's ζ-potential stabilizes, turbidity decreases, and the solution clears. Similar results have been observed in studies by Zhang et al. (2020) with pea protein/gum Arabic and by Aryee et al. (2012) with lentil protein/gum Arabic. These findings suggest that the optimal pH for PPI/β-CD complex coacervation is 4.5, as this pH corresponds to the maximum turbidity, which is indicative of optimal microparticle formation.

In the preparation of PPI/β-CD complexes, the ratio between these two components plays a crucial role in determining the properties of the resulting complex. Turbidity, measured as OD600, serves as a key indicator of the agglomeration process, providing insights into the efficiency of complex formation. As shown in Fig. 1(B), varying the PPI/β-CD ratio alters the optical density at 600 nm, reflecting changes in the interaction between the two molecules. As the PPI concentration increases, the OD600 of the complex solution rises, indicating more aggregation and greater turbidity. However, when the mass ratio between PPI and β-CD reaches 1:1, the turbidity is at its lowest. This suggests that at this ratio, the binding sites on both PPI and β-CD are optimally saturated, leading to a stable complex with minimal free protein or unbound β-CD, reducing further aggregation. Therefore, the 1:1 ratio represents the optimal balance for forming stable PPI/β-CD complexes, minimizing excess aggregation and leading to a well-structured complex. These findings are significant for controlling the structural integrity and functional properties of the complexes, particularly in applications like food systems or drug delivery, where precise particle size and stability are essential. Based on these results, we identified pH = 4.5 as the optimal condition for the coalescence of PPI-β-CD complexes.

Based on the above information, we controlled the pH = 4.5, and the mass ratio of PPI to β-CD was 1:1 to preparation of microcapsules. The effects of fish oil concentration and CQDs concentration on the encapsulation efficiency of microcapsules were investigated, as well as the particle size, ζ-potential, FT-IR and morphology of PPI/FO, β-CD/FO, PPI/β-CD/CQDs/FO microcapsules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of fish oil concentration on microencapsulation encapsulation efficiency and payload
ce:section.ce:para: Higher encapsulation efficiency (EE) is preferred as it indicates a greater proportion of oil encapsulated within the PPI/β-CD coacervate matrix, as compared to the total oil, which includes both surface and encapsulated oils. When the wall material-to-oil ratio was varied from 1:1 to 10:1, as the oil-to-wall material ratio increased, the encapsulation efficiency of the PPI/β-CD microcapsules initially increased by 5.89 %, 15.62 %, and 17.72 %, peaking at 17.07 % before decreasing. This trend occurs because, as the oil concentration decreases, more wall material is absorbed onto the surface of the oil droplets, forming a thicker, denser layer around the oil. This reduces the diffusion of oil to the surface of the particles. However, at the 8:1 ratio, the higher wall material content begins to hinder oil migration, interfering with microcapsule formation and subsequently decreasing the encapsulation efficiency. This finding is consistent with those of Hur et al. (2009).

The ratio of wall materials/oil ratio also influenced the encapsulation of fish oil microcapsules. As shown in Fig. 1(C) (p < 0.5), the payload of fish oil microcapsules prepared with a PPI/β-CD ratio of 6:1 was at least 10.6 % higher than those prepared with a 1:1 ratio. With an increasing wall-to-core ratio, encapsulation efficiency rises continuously. When the ratio of wall to core is 6:1, It indicates that the PPI/B-CD cohesive layer has the best ability to encapsulate fish oil, achieving a maximum encapsulation rate of 85.29 % (Fig. 1(C)). Similar trends have been reported in other studies. Lan et al. (2021) observed that microencapsulated of hemp seed oil with the pea protein isolate-beet pectin complex and found that the encapsulation efficiency increased from 80 % to 96 % with the increase in the wall-to-core ratio from 1:1 to 4:1. These results indicate that PPI/β-CD performs well in terms of encapsulation efficiency and loading rate at a 6:1 wall-to-core ratio, Therefore, based on the data in Fig. 3, we concluded that encapsulation efficiency increases up to a 6:1 ratio, beyond which it decreases. This ratio provides the highest encapsulation rate, and we proceeded to further explore the impact of this ratio on embedding efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs concentration on microencapsulation efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: The encapsulation of fish oil was also influenced by the inclusion of CQDs. with the amount of CQDs added affecting the encapsulation efficiency. CQDs, which are rich in hydroxyl groups, can form hydrogen bonds with PPI/β-CD, creating a more stable complex. This complex can serve as a wall material, enhancing the encapsulation capacity of fish oil. As shown in Fig. 1(D), the encapsulation efficiency of the PPI/β-CD complex increased with the incorporation of CQDs. When the ratio of PPI/β-CD to CQDs was 20:1, the encapsulation rate rose significantly to 86.96 % (p < 0.05). We speculate that this increase is due to the cross-linking of hydroxyl groups with PPI/β-CD, which forms complexes that reduce the leakage of fish oil, thereby improving encapsulation efficiency. Additionally, the CQDs disperse well within the system, filling small pores in the PPI/β-CD complex and enhancing the compactness of the microencapsulated membrane (Wei et al., 2019). However, when the PPI/β-CD to CQDs ratio was reduced to 10:1, the encapsulation rate decreased. As the percentage of CQDs increased, the loading rate began to decline. This may be due to the adsorption capacity of CQDs, where excess CQDs interfere with the ability of PPI/β-CD to encapsulate the fish oil, resulting in a reduced loading rate. These results suggest that adding CQDs can significantly improve the encapsulation efficiency of fish oil microcapsules, with the highest loading rate achieved when the PPI/β-CD to CQDs ratio is 20:1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PPI/FO, β-CD/FO, PPI/β-CD/FO, PPI/β-CD/CQDs/FO particle size, and ζ-potential
ce:section.ce:para: Particle size and ζ potential are important indicators of microcapsule stability. The greater the surface charge of the protein molecules, the stronger the intermolecular repulsion in the solution, which helps prevent protein aggregation. This leads to smaller effective particle sizes and, consequently, greater system stability. Fig. 2 (A) shows the particle size and potential of microcapsules composed of different wall materials. When no CQDs were added, the particle size of PPI microcapsules containing only fish oil was 809 μm, with a ζ-potential of −40.57 mV. For fish oil embedded in β-CD alone, the particle size was 577 μm, and the ζ-potential was −32.67 mV. When fish oil was embedded in a PPI/β-CD composite, the particle size decreased to 602 μm, with a ζ-potential of −30.84 mV. The underlying mechanism is that PPI, as a protein, can exhibit either a net positive or negative charge depending on the pH value, while β-cyclodextrin (β-CD), a polysaccharide, typically carries a weak negative charge. Under optimized pH conditions, the presence of opposite charges between PPI and β-CD facilitates electrostatic complexation, leading to the formation of stable microcapsule walls.

When CQDs were incorporated into the PPI/β-CD complex, the particle size was smaller, and the |ζ| value of the microcapsules significantly increased. The addition of CQDs introduced further negatively charged groups, which enhanced electrostatic interactions within the coacervate matrix and improved microcapsule compactness. This may be due to the negative charge of CQDs neutralizing part of the charge on PPI/β-CD, resulting in a more compact overall structure and a smaller particle size, which enhances system stability (Liang et al., 2022). Huang et al. found that a higher absolute |ζ| potential correlates with better stability of microencapsulated systems. These results indicate that the addition of CQDs not only reduces the average particle size but also enhances the electrostatic stability of the microcapsule, contributing to improved encapsulation efficiency and shelf-life.

The structural state of microcapsule particles is closely related to the flowability of the microcapsule and the ability to protect the core material (Qi et al., 2017). The morphology of microcapsules prepared with different wall materials, as observed by SEM, is shown in Fig. 3. Microcapsules composed of PPI/FO and β-CD/FO exhibited irregular shapes and rough surface. However, the microstructure of the microcapsules was altered following their combination with PPI and β-CD. This shift in structure is consistent with previous studies, such as those by Xiao et al. (2023), which reported similar findings for fish oil encapsulated in PPI/β-CD. The combination of PPI and β-CD improved the integrity of the microcapsules, although some crevices remained, which could negatively affect their protective performance. In contrast, the inclusion of CQDs led to a marked improvement in microcapsule morphology. The CQD-containing samples exhibited more spherical and compact particles with smoother surfaces. Only a few minor surface indentations were observed, and no obvious pores, fractures, or collapses were present. This enhanced structural integrity may be attributed to the abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the CQD surface, which promote stronger hydrogen bonding and crosslinking interactions between PPI and β-CD, resulting in a denser and more cohesive wall matrix. These results suggest that the PPI/β-CD/CQDs coacervate-produced microcapsules exhibit the most desirable form and effectively shield the fish oil inside. Therefore, the group containing CQDs demonstrated the best microencapsulation integrity, which aligns with the previous conclusions regarding particle size and ζ-potential.

FTIR analysis was performed to further explore the chemical structure of PPI, β-CD, CQDs, FO, and their interactions during complex coacervation and microencapsulation (Chen et al., 2022). As shown in Fig. 2(B), the infrared spectrum of PPI displays a broad absorption peak at 3310 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of -OH, a peak at 1636 cm−1 for the stretching vibration of C=O or the antisymmetric carboxyl group, a C-H bending vibration at 1460 cm−1, and a C-N stretching vibration at 1146 cm−1. The β-CD spectrum shows key peaks at 3333 cm−1 and 1649 cm−1, corresponding to O-H and H-O-H stretching modes, respectively. In the infrared spectrum of fish oil, the peaks at 2923 cm−1 and 2857 cm−1 represent the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the C-H bond in CH2, while the peak at 1744 cm−1 is attributed to the C=O stretching vibration in the fatty acid ester bond. A peak at 1465 cm−1 corresponds to the shear vibration of the C-H bond in the fatty acid, and the peaks around 1158 cm−1 represent the stretching vibrations of C-O and C-O-C in the ester bond. The peak at 717 cm−1 indicates the out-of-plane bending vibration of the C-H bond in CH2.

The spectrum of PPI/β-CD/CQDs microcapsules shows increased band intensity between 3000 and 2800 cm−1, particularly at 2923 cm−1, suggesting a higher presence of unsaturated fatty acids in the PPI/β-CD/CQDs microcapsules. After freeze-drying, the FTIR spectra of the PPI/β-CD and PPI/β-CD/CQDs composite wall materials retained characteristic absorption peaks from both the wall materials and the fish oil, confirming successful encapsulation. Lin et al. (2021) observed a similar phenomenon when studying the ovalbumin/alginate as the wall, which the characteristic peaks for fish oil encapsulated with ovalbumin/alginate as wall materials did not change significantly, indicating that the microencapsulation process did not alter the properties of the fish oil. Additionally, the ester groups (C=O and C-O-C) related to fish oil, observed at 2857.50 cm−1 and 1744 cm−1, showed the characteristic peaks are weakened and shifted, which shows that the composite wall material. The broader absorption peak at 3410 cm−1 suggests the formation of new hydrogen bonds due to covalent cross-linking between the core and the wall material of the microcapsules. These changes in the characteristic absorption peaks of fish oil functional groups in the microencapsulation spectra indicate that PPI, β-CD, and CQDs effectively encapsulated the fish oil as part of the composite wall material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Changes of POV and TBA of microcapsules with different wall materials during fish oil storage
ce:section.ce:para: To specifically analyze the changes in microcapsule stability with different wall materials, we examined the POV and TBA values of four groups of fish oil microcapsules and fish oil in 4 °C, with the results shown in Fig. 4 . POV is an important index that characterizes the degree of oxidation in oils and can indicate whether fish oil has undergone rancidity or degradation (Luo et al., 2023). TBA, on the other hand, is used to determine the secondary oxidation products in oils, particularly malondialdehyde (Nida Demirtas Erol et al., 2022). As shown in Fig. 4(A) and (B), both POV and TBA values increased over time for all groups (Yeşilsu et al., 2023). However, the increase in POV(4.1156 meq/kg) and TBA (0.0230 mg/kg) for microencapsulated fish oils was slower compared to unencapsulated oils, suggesting that microencapsulation significantly enhanced the stability of the fish oils. Among the different wall materials, the POV of microcapsules encapsulated with PPI/β-CD/CQDs was significantly lower than that of the other groups, indicating that the addition of CQDs led to a more compact encapsulation. This finding is consistent with the results shown in Fig. 3(D), where the higher morphological integrity of the microcapsules resulted in reduced oxidation of the fish oils, thereby achieving the lowest POV values. Compared with the study by Wang et al. (2025) the microcapsules prepared in this study have lower POV and higher stability. In nutritional fortifiers, the peroxide value of fish oil should be ≤ 5.0 meq/kg.

Similarly, Fig. 4(B) shows the TBA values for the different groups, which gradually increased with storage time. However, the increase in TBA was smallest for the PPI/β-CD/CQDs microcapsules, suggesting that this wall material was most effective in protecting the fish oils from exposure to air, thereby reducing the production of malondialdehyde, a marker of lipid oxidation. By comparing the changes in POV for fish oil microcapsules embedded with different wall materials, it is evident that the PPI/β-CD/CQDs microcapsules exhibited significantly lower POV values at the later stages of storage (p < 0.05), likely due to the antioxidant properties of CQDs (Zhao et al., 2022). This antioxidant effect is attributed to the presence of surface hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine groups on CQDs, which can scavenge free radicals and chelate pro-oxidant metal ions, thereby interrupting lipid peroxidation chain reactions and stabilizing fish oil during storage (Roy et al., 2024). Therefore, microencapsulation effectively seals the fish oil within the wall material, reducing its exposure to air and preventing oxidation, which significantly improves its storage stability. The PPI/β-CD/CQDs encapsulated fish oil showed the best antioxidant stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size of PPI/β-CD/CQDs microcapsules and release properties of fish oil microcapsules in in vitro simulation tests
ce:section.ce:para: The changes in particle size and ζ-potential can reflect the behavior of microcapsules during simulated digestion. During simulated digestion, an increase in particle size typically indicates aggregation, swelling, or partial disintegration of the microcapsule matrix, reflecting a loss of structural integrity. Additionally, a significant decrease in the absolute value of the ζ-potential suggests a reduction in electrostatic repulsion, which can lead to destabilization and subsequent breakdown of the microcapsule structure. As shown in Fig. 5 (A) and (B), both particle size and ζ-potential were altered throughout the simulated digestion process. The particle size of the microcapsule recovery solution was smaller, and its ζ-potential differed significantly from those of the oral saliva and gastric fluid digestates, with a ζ-potential value of −38.24 mV (p < 0.5), indicating that the recovery solution had better stability. After simulated oral saliva digestion, the particle size of the microcapsules increased, and the ζ-potential also increased. This is likely because proteins in the microcapsule wall material interacted with the salt ions in the saliva, forming flocculent aggregates that increased the particle size. The presence of salt ions in saliva likely contributed to this increase in ζ-potential as well. Subsequent digestion by simulated gastric fluid further changed the particle size, which was significantly larger (p < 0.5) compared to the other simulated digestion systems. his change can be attributed to pre-digestion hydration, which caused the microcapsules to swell and increase in volume. The high concentration of salt ions in gastric fluid also enhanced aggregation and flocculation, further increasing the particle size (Ahamd et al., 2017). The smaller absolute ζ-potential observed in gastric fluid digestion is likely due to partial hydrolysis of the microcapsule wall proteins by pepsin, which leads to a positive charge on most of the proteins. These positively charged proteins repel the negative charges on the microcapsule surface, reducing the absolute ζ-potential (Tian et al., 2024). During digestion with simulated intestinal fluids, the microcapsule structure was disrupted, leading to a significant decrease in particle size (p < 0.5), indicating near-complete dissolution of the microcapsules. However, the ζ-potential of the microcapsules significantly increased (p < 0.5) after intestinal digestion. This increase is likely due to the action of bile salts and pancreatic enzymes, which replace the original proteins with some negatively charged surfactants, stabilizing the structure of the microcapsules.

As shown in Fig. 5 (C), the effect of wall material type on the release of fish oil from microcapsules during in vitro gastrointestinal digestion is illustrated. A small amount of free fatty acids (FFA) was released from the fish oil microcapsules upon introduction into the intestinal fluid digestion solution. In the early stages of intestinal digestion, the rate of FFA release increased over time, with a significant magnitude. This initial release likely results from the digestion of residual surface oils on the microcapsules. However, as digestion progressed, the rate of FFA release slowed down. By the later stages of intestinal digestion, the release reached 82.09 %, but was not fully completed. This incomplete release may be due to the limited digestive capacity of the simulated oral saliva and gastric juices on the microcapsule wall material. The microcapsule wall, composed of a sugar-protein complex, has a dense structure that gastric juice cannot fully digest, thus preventing complete breakdown of the microcapsule wall. Xiao et al. (2024) found that adding oxidized konjac glucomannan to the wall material increased the in vitro digestibility of walnut oil microcapsules from 45.79 % to 54.85 %. This indicates that composite wall materials with stronger resistance to digestive enzymes can effectively protect the encapsulated fish oil and control its release until it reaches the intestinal fluid. In addition, previous studies have shown that the introduction of CQDs does not pose potential toxicity or food safety risks.

In this study, fish oil was successfully encapsulated using PPI, β-CD, and CQDs as wall materials through a freeze-drying technique, resulting in microcapsules with excellent stability and structural integrity. The optimal pH for the formation of the PPI/β-CD coacervate, along with its particle size and ζ-potential, were first investigated. Furthermore, the optimal core-to-wall ratio (1:6) and the most effective CQDs addition ratio (20:1) were determined for the composite wall materials. Compared with other groups, microcapsules prepared with PPI/β-CD/CQDs demonstrated enhanced stability, effectively delaying increases in POV and TBA values under elevated temperature and prolonged storage conditions. Additionally, simulated gastrointestinal digestion studies further demonstrated that microencapsulation facilitated the controlled release and enhanced intestinal absorption of fish oil. Therefore, the PPI/β-CD/CQDs-based microcapsules effectively protected fish oil against oxidation and degradation during storage, making them a promising solution for encapsulating sensitive bioactive compounds like fish oil.

Wenyuchu Chen: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Fang Tian: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yongjing Xu: Investigation, Data curation. Yujin Li: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Weiliang Guan: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Chenyang Lu: Formal analysis. Sheng Hu: Software, Formal analysis. Luyun Cai: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
Key Science & Technology Program of Wenzhou City (ZN2021002); Key R&D Program of Ningbo City (2024Z277; 2024Z282)

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have influenced the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: PPI, pea protein isolates; β-CD, β-cyclodextrin; CQDs, carbon quantum dots; POV, peroxide value; TBA, thiobarbituric acid; FFA, free fatty acids.
